AI生成広告はどう見分ける？「どう作られたか」表示を読み解く実践ガイド: How to Read AI-Generated Ads: A Practical Guide to Transparency Notices
AIで作成・編集された広告が増える今、広告の「どう作られたか」表示を手がかりに、: A practical guide to using an ad’s creation disclosure to check its source, claims and purchase conditions before you act.
ai_ad_transparency.title: How to Read AI-Generated Ads: A Practical Guide to Transparency Notices
ai_ad_transparency.heading: What to check first when an ad may use AI
ai_ad_transparency.description: A practical guide to using an ad’s creation disclosure to check its source, claims and purchase conditions before you act.
ai_ad_transparency.keywords: AI-generated ads, AI advertising, ad transparency, how an ad was made, purchase check, digital literacy
ai_ad_transparency.body:
The more polished an advert looks, the more readily we may assume that the product must look exactly the same. Generative AI now makes it quick to replace a background, refine a person or create a scene that was never photographed. That is not a reason to treat every AI-assisted advert as suspect. It is a reason to separate an image’s persuasive effect from the facts that can support a buying decision. Let a striking visual catch your attention, then let a second, calmer check decide whether you purchase, subscribe or share your details.

An AI disclosure is not a danger label

AI-assisted advertising can help small businesses explain an idea without the cost of a large shoot. Knowing that AI was used does not, by itself, tell you whether a product is good or a claim is true. The real risk is mistaking an edited scene for a product promise. On 9 July 2026, Google announced expanded disclosures in Search, YouTube and Discover through a “How this ad was made” panel, intended to make AI creation or editing easier to understand. Availability and placement can change, but the useful habit is stable: look for context before you draw a conclusion from a picture.

A 30-second check before buying

First, open the disclosure. If an ad menu offers ad information or a creation notice, use it to understand the image—not to pass judgement on the advertiser. Next, leave the ad to verify the seller. Find the company name, contact route, returns policy and terms on the seller’s own site. Compare them with search results, map listings and independent reviews; unexplained differences in name or address are worth pausing over. Finally, read the promise in words. Check price, delivery, recurring charges, cancellation, warranty and eligibility in the product page or checkout text. The stronger the phrase—“limited,” “lowest price,” “guaranteed”—the more carefully its conditions and evidence deserve to be read.

Move your attention from the image to the evidence

Odd hands, impossible shadows or broken lettering can sometimes reveal a synthetic image. But spotting visual flaws is not the goal. Convincing images and ordinary photographs alike can leave out material details. When an offer matters, move beyond the image to the specification, customer photos, support contact and the exact return or cancellation path. Treat health, beauty, investment, employment and child-related ads with extra care: an ad should begin research, not end it.

Use transparency as room to think

A disclosure about AI use is not a verdict on an advertiser. It gives you a small pause in which to ask: what is presentation, and what can I independently confirm? That pause protects your ability to choose. You do not need to reject a beautiful ad; you only need to check the seller, the conditions and the supporting evidence before pressing the button.

Current source consulted

Google: Expanding AI transparency in ads, published 9 July 2026. This article independently develops a consumer-facing verification routine from that announcement.
